items.prompt_md: In the transpose-then-reverse rotation, what does the transpose step alone accomplish?
options: It rotates the matrix by 90 degrees directly
It swaps `matrix[i][j]` with `matrix[j][i]` for every `i < j`, mirroring the matrix across its main diagonal
It reverses the order of the columns
It sorts each row into ascending order
items.answer: It swaps `matrix[i][j]` with `matrix[j][i]` for every `i < j`, mirroring the matrix across its main diagonal
items.explanation_md: Transpose only mirrors the grid across the main diagonal; a second pass (reversing every row) is what turns that mirror into an actual 90-degree rotation.

items.prompt_md: After transposing a square matrix, why does reversing every row (rather than every column) complete a clockwise 90-degree rotation?
options: Reversing rows moves each transposed value into the column position a clockwise turn would put it in, since transpose already handled the row/column flip
Reversing rows undoes the transpose, so the two steps cancel out
Reversing rows sorts values, which happens to look like a rotation for most inputs
It only works for counterclockwise rotation, not clockwise
items.answer: Reversing rows moves each transposed value into the column position a clockwise turn would put it in, since transpose already handled the row/column flip
items.explanation_md: Transpose puts each value on the correct diagonal-relative axis; reversing each row then slides values left-to-right into exactly the column each one needs for a clockwise turn.

items.prompt_md: The 4-way cycle swap approach rotates a matrix without a separate transpose pass. What does it actually do?
options: It walks each ring of the matrix and, for every group of four positions related by a 90-degree turn, cycles their four values in one shot
It builds a brand-new matrix and copies each value into its rotated slot
It reverses every row first, then reverses every column
It sorts each ring's values before placing them back
items.answer: It walks each ring of the matrix and, for every group of four positions related by a 90-degree turn, cycles their four values in one shot
items.explanation_md: Instead of two full passes, the 4-way cycle directly moves each of the four corresponding cells (top, right, bottom, left of a ring) into each other's rotated slot using a handful of temp variables.

items.prompt_md: Why is allocating a brand-new matrix to hold the rotated result considered wasteful, even though it's simpler to write?
options: It still visits every cell once for time, but now also needs a second full grid, costing O(n^2) extra space instead of O(1)
It is asymptotically slower in time than transpose-then-reverse
It only works for matrices with an even number of rows
It cannot correctly compute the rotated position for any cell
items.answer: It still visits every cell once for time, but now also needs a second full grid, costing O(n^2) extra space instead of O(1)
items.explanation_md: Both approaches touch every cell exactly once, so time stays the same; the copy-to-new-grid approach simply spends memory it doesn't need to.

items.prompt_md: Why does the transpose-then-reverse rotation require the matrix to be square (n x n)?
options: Transposing swaps row and column counts, so an m x n matrix would become n x m — a shape the original m x n buffer cannot hold in place
Reversing rows only works correctly when there is an even number of rows
Square matrices are the only ones where every cell contains a number
It is a stylistic convention, not a real constraint
items.answer: Transposing swaps row and column counts, so an m x n matrix would become n x m — a shape the original m x n buffer cannot hold in place
items.explanation_md: In-place transpose relies on swapping symmetric positions within the same fixed-size grid; a non-square matrix changes dimensions on rotation, so it can't be rewritten into the same backing array without extra storage.

items.prompt_md: In spiral-order traversal, what do the four boundary variables (`top`, `bottom`, `left`, `right`) represent?
options: The rows and columns of the still-unvisited rectangle remaining in the matrix
The four corner values of the matrix
A record of every cell already visited, used to avoid duplicates
The starting and ending index of the current row only
items.answer: The rows and columns of the still-unvisited rectangle remaining in the matrix
items.explanation_md: Each boundary marks one edge of the shrinking rectangle that hasn't been walked yet; after each of the four edges is traversed, its boundary steps inward, shrinking the remaining rectangle.

items.prompt_md: Why must spiral traversal re-check the boundary condition (e.g., `top <= bottom`) between each of the four edge walks within a single ring, rather than only once per ring?
options: Because walking earlier edges in the same ring can shrink the rectangle down to a single remaining row or column, and re-checking prevents revisiting or reading cells that no longer exist
Because the check is purely stylistic and has no effect on correctness
Because the matrix might change size while being traversed
Because integer overflow occurs if the check happens more than once
items.answer: Because walking earlier edges in the same ring can shrink the rectangle down to a single remaining row or column, and re-checking prevents revisiting or reading cells that no longer exist
items.explanation_md: On a rectangular matrix, the top and bottom edges of the final ring can collapse into the same row (or left/right into the same column) partway through a ring, so skipping the mid-ring check causes double-counted or out-of-range cells.

items.prompt_md: In the set-matrix-zeroes technique, why use the first row and first column of the matrix itself as markers instead of a separate hash set of zero positions?
options: Writing markers into the first row/column reuses existing storage, achieving O(1) extra space, whereas a hash set of positions grows with the number of zeroes found
A hash set cannot store row/column indices
The first row and column are guaranteed to already be all zero before the algorithm starts
It makes the algorithm run in O(1) time instead of O(m*n)
items.answer: Writing markers into the first row/column reuses existing storage, achieving O(1) extra space, whereas a hash set of positions grows with the number of zeroes found
items.explanation_md: The first row and column are cells the algorithm already owns, so marking `matrix[i][0]` and `matrix[0][j]` costs no extra memory, unlike a hash set whose size scales with how many zero rows/columns exist.

items.prompt_md: Both the row-zero check and the column-zero check for the first row/column want to use `matrix[0][0]` as their marker. What bug does this create if you don't handle it carefully, and how is it usually fixed?
options: Overwriting `matrix[0][0]` for one check can clobber or be double-negated by the other, so the fix is to track whether row 0 and column 0 originally contained a zero using two separate boolean flags recorded before any marking begins
There is no real conflict, since `matrix[0][0]` can safely hold two independent marker values at once
The fix is to skip marking row 0 and column 0 entirely and zero them unconditionally at the end
The conflict only appears when the matrix has exactly one row and one column, so it can be ignored in general
items.answer: Overwriting `matrix[0][0]` for one check can clobber or be double-negated by the other, so the fix is to track whether row 0 and column 0 originally contained a zero using two separate boolean flags recorded before any marking begins
items.explanation_md: `matrix[0][0]` is shared between the 'row 0 needs zeroing' signal and the 'column 0 needs zeroing' signal; letting one check's write flip or re-flip the other's marker loses information, so each needs its own flag captured up front.

items.prompt_md: A buggy spiral-traversal implementation shrinks `left` and `right` inward after the top and bottom edges, but forgets to re-check `left <= right` before walking the bottom edge on a matrix with a single remaining column. What goes wrong?
options: The bottom-edge walk can revisit the same single column already covered by the top-edge walk, producing a duplicate or out-of-range read
The traversal terminates immediately without visiting any cells
The matrix values become corrupted because a write occurs instead of a read
The time complexity becomes O(n^2) instead of O(m*n)
items.answer: The bottom-edge walk can revisit the same single column already covered by the top-edge walk, producing a duplicate or out-of-range read
items.explanation_md: Once the remaining rectangle collapses to one row or one column, later edge walks in the same ring must check the boundary again — otherwise a cell already visited by an earlier edge gets visited (or read past the true edge) a second time.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the in-place transpose-then-reverse rotation for an n x n matrix?
options: O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space
O(n^2) time, O(n^2) extra space
O(n) time, O(1) extra space
O(n^3) time, O(1) extra space
items.answer: O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: Every cell is touched once during transpose and once during row reversal, giving O(n^2) time; both passes only swap values already inside the matrix, so extra space stays O(1).

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of spiral-order traversal over an m x n matrix using the four shrinking boundary pointers, not counting the output list of visited values?
options: O(m*n) time, O(1) extra space
O(m*n) time, O(m*n) extra space
O(m+n) time, O(1) extra space
O(m*n) time, O(m+n) extra space
items.answer: O(m*n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: Each of the m*n cells is visited exactly once as the boundaries shrink inward, and only the four integer boundary variables are needed beyond the output — O(m*n) time, O(1) extra space.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the set-matrix-zeroes technique that uses the first row and column as markers?
options: O(m*n) time, O(1) extra space
O(m*n) time, O(m+n) extra space
O(m*n) time, O(m*n) extra space
O((m*n)^2) time, O(1) extra space
items.answer: O(m*n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: The matrix is scanned a constant number of times (to mark, then to apply markers), so time is O(m*n); because markers live inside the existing first row/column plus two booleans, extra space is O(1).

items.prompt_md: You're given a square image stored as a 2D array and must rotate it 90 degrees clockwise without allocating a second grid. Which approach fits?
options: Copy to a new matrix
In-place transpose + reverse
Hash set of zero positions
First row/col as markers
items.answer: In-place transpose + reverse
items.explanation_md: Rotating a grid in place with O(1) extra space is exactly the transpose-then-reverse trigger; copying to a new matrix or using auxiliary hash structures both cost memory the constraint forbids.

items.prompt_md: You must rotate a square matrix 90 degrees in place, and you want to do it as a single pass per ring rather than two full passes over the whole grid. Which approach fits?
options: In-place transpose + reverse
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
Copy to a new matrix
Hash set of zero positions
items.answer: 4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
items.explanation_md: Cycling each ring's four corresponding cells directly performs the rotation ring-by-ring in one combined pass, unlike transpose-then-reverse which sweeps the whole grid twice; both stay in-place, but the cycle walk matches the 'one pass per ring' requirement.

items.prompt_md: You need to visit every cell of an m x n grid in a single continuous loop that spirals from the outer edge inward, collecting the values in that order. Which approach fits?
options: In-place transpose + reverse
First row/col as markers
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
Hash set of zero positions
items.answer: 4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
items.explanation_md: Walking the top, right, bottom, and left edges of a shrinking rectangle, stepping each boundary inward after its edge is done, is precisely how a spiral order is produced — transpose/reverse and marker tricks solve different problems entirely.

items.prompt_md: You must zero out every row and column that contains at least one zero in an m x n matrix, and you're told extra memory must stay O(1) regardless of matrix size. Which approach fits?
options: Copy to a new matrix
Hash set of zero positions
First row/col as markers
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
items.answer: First row/col as markers
items.explanation_md: Recording which rows/columns need zeroing inside the matrix's own first row and column costs no extra memory; a hash set or a full copy both grow with the input size, violating the O(1) constraint.

items.prompt_md: You need to zero out rows/columns containing zeroes, but this time extra memory proportional to the number of zero rows and columns found is explicitly acceptable, and you'd rather keep the code simple than shave every byte. Which approach fits best?
options: First row/col as markers
Hash set of zero positions
In-place transpose + reverse
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
items.answer: Hash set of zero positions
items.explanation_md: Once the O(1)-space constraint is lifted, tracking zero rows/columns in a plain hash set is simpler to reason about than reusing the first row/column as markers, and the marker technique's main advantage — saved memory — no longer matters.

items.prompt_md: You're handed a rectangular (non-square) matrix and asked to produce its 90-degree rotation, and the interviewer clarifies that the result may be written into a freshly allocated buffer rather than reusing the original array. Which approach fits best?
options: In-place transpose + reverse
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
Copy to a new matrix
First row/col as markers
items.answer: Copy to a new matrix
items.explanation_md: A rectangular matrix changes shape (m x n becomes n x m) on rotation, so it cannot be rewritten into the same backing array; once a new buffer is explicitly allowed, computing each cell's rotated destination directly into it is simplest.

items.prompt_md: You must rotate a square matrix 90 degrees counterclockwise in place, with no second grid allowed. Which approach fits?
options: In-place transpose + reverse, adapted with the diagonal/reverse order swapped for the opposite rotation direction
Copy to a new matrix, writing each value directly at its counterclockwise destination
Hash set of zero positions
First row/col as markers
items.answer: In-place transpose + reverse, adapted with the diagonal/reverse order swapped for the opposite rotation direction
items.explanation_md: The same in-place index-transform idea handles the opposite rotation direction — only the order of the diagonal flip and reversal (or which axis gets reversed) changes; the O(1)-space in-place trigger is unchanged.

items.prompt_md: You need a list of the exact (row, col) coordinates of every zero-valued cell in a matrix, to use later for an unrelated computation — not simply to zero out their rows and columns. Which approach fits?
options: First row/col as markers
In-place transpose + reverse
Hash set of zero positions
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
items.answer: Hash set of zero positions
items.explanation_md: The marker technique deliberately discards individual zero coordinates, keeping only 'this row/column has a zero somewhere' — when the actual positions are needed downstream, they must be collected explicitly, such as into a set or list.

items.prompt_md: You're asked to traverse a grid boundary-by-boundary from the outside in, but the grid is guaranteed square and small enough that building an entire second grid to hold a 'visited' flag per cell is acceptable and the interviewer only cares that the output order is correct. Which approach still fits best?
options: Copy to a new matrix
4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
Hash set of zero positions
First row/col as markers
items.answer: 4-way boundary cycle / spiral with shrinking bounds
items.explanation_md: Even when extra memory is affordable, the four shrinking boundaries are still the natural way to produce spiral order directly; a visited-flag grid only adds bookkeeping without changing how the traversal order itself is computed.

items.prompt_md: You must transform a 2D matrix in place — rotating it or walking it in a defined traversal order — under a strict O(1) extra-space requirement, and the matrix's structure lets you express the transform as a couple of named index operations rather than a formula for every cell at once. Which class of approach fits this situation generally?
options: Copy to a new matrix
Hash set of zero positions
In-place index-math passes (transpose + reverse, or shrinking boundary walk) over the existing grid
Backtracking over all possible cell orderings
items.answer: In-place index-math passes (transpose + reverse, or shrinking boundary walk) over the existing grid
items.explanation_md: The trigger — transform or traverse a grid in place with O(1) extra space, decomposed into simple reusable index operations — is exactly the transpose/reverse or boundary-shrink family, not a new-grid copy, a hash structure, or brute-force search.
